narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 missense variant Q687P (c.2060A>C, p.Gln687Pro) has been identified in several cancer‑related contexts, most notably in ovarian tumour sequencing panels and in a patient with bilateral Wilms tumour, and is listed in a pancreatic cancer precision‑medicine review.
Clinical reports predominantly classify the change as a variant of uncertain significance. In a multiplex PCR‑NGS study of FFPE ovarian tumours the variant was detected and reported as VUS [PMC4391122]. A germline analysis of bilateral Wilms tumour patients also described the same missense change as VUS [PMC10728430], and a recent review of BRCA‑mutated pancreatic cancer listed Q687P among missense variants of unknown impact [PMC8869830]. One study, however, described the allele as a pathogenic missense variant in a Wilms tumour case [PMC10055651], creating a discordant interpretation.
No functional assays or experimental data addressing the effect of the Q687P substitution on BRCA1 protein function have been reported; the only functional‑type information is the demonstration that the variant can be reliably detected by the NGS workflow used in the ovarian tumour study [PMC4391122].
Population frequency data and in‑silico predictive scores are not provided in the extracted evidence.
Taken together, the preponderance of evidence classifies BRCA1 Q687P as a variant of uncertain significance, with a single report suggesting pathogenicity but lacking supporting functional or population data. Consequently, the current weight of evidence does not permit a definitive pathogenic or benign assignment.

As none of the tumour samples were found to contain a validated low frequency variant, we used a series of decreasing variant content admixtures as a model system to assess the ability of the method to detect variants at low proportions as could occur in a heterogeneous tumour. Three FFPE tumour DNA samples known to harbour BRCA pathogenic mutations were mixed with a single background non-mutant FFPE tumour DNA to make 1/2, 1/4, 1/8 and 1/16 admixtures. The total amplifiable input DNA remained constant at 80 ng per test (20 ng per plex) (Admix set 1: BRCA2 c.10095delinsGAATTATATCT p.(Ser3366AsnfsTer4); Admix set 2: BRCA1 **c.2060A>C** p.(**Gln687Pro**); Admix set 3: BRCA2 c.7007+1G>C). All the variants were clearly detected in the lowest 1/16 admixture (Table 3). Although the relative numbers of reads with the variants declined with the lower admixtures, the actual proportion was not always as predicted and may indicate that the measure of amplifiable DNA at 129 bp qPCR is not fully reflecting the numbers of amplifiable copies of each BRCA locus in each sample. Nevertheless, BRCA mutations could be readily detected if present in >10% of a sample DNA, which should enable the detection of low level mutations commonly found in tumour material. If the level of neoplastic cells is lower, macro or microdissection to enrich for the proportion of tumour cells may be necessary to achieve the required limit of detection although we did not assess this in this study.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article shows that the multiplex PCR‑NGS workflow can reliably detect the BRCA1 Q687P (c.2060A>C) variant in FFPE tumour tissue, but classifies it as a variant of uncertain significance, providing no functional or clinical impact assessment.
article_mentions.title: A reliable method for the detection of BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations in fixed tumour tissue utilising multiplex PCR-based targeted next generation sequencing

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review outlines current and emerging precision‑medicine approaches for BRCA1/2‑ or PALB2‑mutated pancreatic cancer, highlighting PARP inhibitor therapy for HR‑deficient tumors.
article_mentions.title: Precision Medicine for BRCA/PALB2-Mutated Pancreatic Cancer and Emerging Strategies to Improve Therapeutic Responses to PARP Inhibition

article_mentions.title: Genetic and epigenetic features of bilateral Wilms tumor predisposition in patients from the Children's Oncology Group AREN18B5-Q
